does: This paper identifies a critical disconnect between calibration quality and actual decision-making reliability in machine unlearning scenarios. The authors evaluate models on the TOFU benchmark using multiple-choice QA tasks, measuring calibration through standard metrics (ECE, MCE, Brier score) alongside decision-rule reliability via attribution-based shortcut detection using Integrated Gradients and Local Mutual Information. The core finding is that fine-tuned models achieve very low calibration error (ECE ~0.04) compared to pretrained models (ECE >0.5), and crucially, models after unlearning maintain similarly low calibration despite reduced accuracy on forgotten data, while attribution analysis reveals increased reliance on spurious correlations rather than genuine reasoning.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature, which traditionally focuses on removing training data influence while preserving model performance. It responds to and challenges the widespread assumption that calibration serves as a reliable proxy for model trustworthiness, building on decades of uncertainty quantification research in machine learning. The paper draws on attribution-based analysis methods from interpretability research and connects to recent work on shortcut learning and spurious correlations in neural networks. It engages with evaluation methodology debates in unlearning, where practitioners often rely on calibration metrics without questioning whether low calibration error actually indicates safe, reliable decision-making.
enables: This work opens paths toward more rigorous evaluation protocols for machine unlearning and other scenarios where model reliability matters critically. It motivates the development of compound evaluation frameworks that combine calibration metrics with decision-rule analysis rather than treating calibration as sufficient. Future work can explore whether the calibration-reliability gap holds across different model architectures, unlearning methods, or domains beyond QA. The findings suggest new research directions in designing unlearning objectives that explicitly target decision-rule reliability, and in developing improved attribution methods or uncertainty estimates that better capture whether models use genuine knowledge versus spurious patterns for their predictions.
